track_listing.track: Lights Out: (v/a LP - Live N Heavy) NEmS
Layla (LP - Layla And Other Assorted Love Songs) ATCO
Live Now - Pay Later (LP - Girls To Chat & Boys To Bounce) Bearsville
Lady Luck (LP - Take No Prisoners) Epic
Macho City (LP - Circle Of Love) Mercury
One Hell Of A Night (v/a LP - The Friday Rock Show) BBC
So Young So Bad (LP - Mayday) A&M
Father Of Day Father Of Night
Can't Happen Here (LP - Best Of Rainbow) Polydor

transcript.text: On 275 and 285. Also in stereo on VHF. This is National Radio 1.
Well, hello there. This is TV on the Radio here, Thomas the Vance. And welcome once again to The Friday Rock Show.
Okay, tonight out of the archives, some cuts that were recorded by Manfred Mann back in 1973.
And also a session that stemmed from a demo tape that we heard that featured just one guy doing everything.
He now calls his band Euphoria.
On top of that, I've got some new releases by Neil Young, Foggart, Molly Hatchett, Gillan, Rose Tattoo, Steve Miller, Demon and Mayday.
Some classic cuts by Derek and the Dominoes, Rainbow and Motorhead, amongst others, who will be featured in the Friday Night Connection.
The answer to last week's and a brand new one.
But this evening, we start with this.
That was Neil Young. It's a track from his new album, which is called Reactor.
It's out at the moment on Reprise Records.
The track you heard was called Shots.
and it featured the usual amazing amount of distortion
that that guy gets out of his guitar.
How many amplifiers he's blown up in his career
is nobody's business.
And before that, something from a new album
which is out on NEMS Records.
It's a compilation called Live and Heavy.
The track we played, of course, was by UFO,
a really good version of Lights Out in London
recorded in Chicago in 1978.
But also on the compilation you will find
Deep Purple, Nazareth, Motorhead,
Def Leppard, Rainbow, Gillen, Black Sabbath,
Whitesnake and Status Quo.
It's a good live compilation.
And now the time is 16 minutes after 10.
This is the Friday Rock Show.
It's warm in here, and I hope it's warm wherever you are,
and you're not totally snowbound.
And also, if you're driving, please take care,
for and on behalf of yourself and everybody else as well.
We heard a demo tape some time ago that was recorded in somebody's bedroom,
actually in the bedroom of Peter Layden.
And we were quite impressed with it,
because he plays a really sort of nice Hendrix guitar.
Now, for the purposes of this session by Mr. Peter Layden,
He was joined by Ken Donaldson on the bass
and also Alan Hepplewhite on the drums.
We think it's good. Hope you like it.
Eric and the Dominoes, alias, of course, Eric Clapton and Layla.
Especially for a fellow by the name of Rob or Beaker,
I think that's his other name, from Graham,
whose other name is Titch, and they live down in Bristol.
Congratulations, Beaker, on passing your major exam
in your telecommunications course.
And also, that track was requested by Temandra Harkness,
who lives in Market Drayton in Shropshire.
She wants me to play it for Nick and Jen
and everybody who went to the Bath CND train,
or rather went on that train to London.
The Hayesfield Sixth Form and Mary King,
a really nice family from Islington.
She met on a train from Paris.
All ex-peoples of Lecoq, the Grove Sixth Form,
the Stag in Drayton, the Twilight Zone,
and the admissions tutors of various university drama departments.
Also Newcastle Poly and Bullmarsh College,
and all her relations as well.
And before that, the first of four from Euphoria,
which I'd like to dedicate to a gentleman called Michael Broadley
who should have gone to see Dylan last night in Derby.
I'm also going to say hello to all the headbangers in 5B
at the Nottingham Bluecoat School,
especially Mark and Jock and Neville and David and Chris and Clive.
And finally, hello to Jan and Viv of Winchmore Hill in North London.
10.28 and a half. This is Foggart.
The rookie band from down south in the United States of America
by the name of Molly Hatchet
and a track called Lady Luck from their new album
which is out on Epic.
The album is called Take No Prisoners.
And before that, something from a new album
which is out on Bearsville.
It features the band called Foggart.
The album's called Girls to Chat and Boys to Bounce.
And the track that I played you was called
Live Now and Pay Later.
They were formed, Foggart were formed back in about 1972.
They were an offshoot from the band called Savoy Brown.
And they were put together really by lonesome Dave Peveritt.
And they're much, much bigger to this day in America
than they are over here, which is quite surprising.
I once did a TV show with them.
I think it was about 1973 or 1974 over here
at the Rainbow for the United States of America,
and I thought they were really impressive.
Although some of their latest album is a little bit poppy.
Nonetheless, I'd like to dedicate both those tracks to the following people.
Steve Sweeney, who lives in Larnon County, Antrim in Northern Ireland.
I know he's a froggat freak.
He wanted me to say hello to Humpy and Boise and Hub,
and thanks Joe for the regular Friday night rock sessions
in No. 1 Tor Gardens, he says here.
And also hello to Marie and Yvonne and Tony and Paul and Mark and me,
me being Carolyn, who's up there in Bonny Dundee in Scotland,
who says say hi to Gillan at the ice rink.
And also hello to a lot of people who are disappointed
because they're not going to be able to see Ozzy Osbourne this time around,
including Simon Wakely and all the headbangers in the fifth form
at the Nottingham Bluecoat School.
I think I've already done a card from them, but we still will continue.
Especially Jock and Mark and Chris and Richard and I and Neville and David.
Into the archives circa 1973, recorded on the 3rd of the 4th, 1973, this is Manfred Mann.
Recorded way back in 1973, that was Manfred Mann, about a year after he formed his Earth Band.
And when you look in the books, the sort of study-up books relating to rock and roll,
you suddenly discover or reacquaint yourself of the fact that he really has worked with so many great British musicians over the years.
Of course, he himself was born in South Africa.

was Bubblegum and Kipling.

and I hope you are.

that you'd like us to try and find in the vaults of the BBC,
just let us know, and Mr. Wilson and myself will do our very best for you.
